Gotcha, so this formulation of degenerate faces is saying, "both edges are longer than the tolerance inside unitize, and the dot product of one onto the other is larger than tol.zero." Seems reasonable- also I changed the threshold to be settable in unitize in a branch. 

Though doesn't this work out to the same thing as the original one which was "one edge and the height of the triangle are greater than tol.merge"? The dot product is the height, and the edge length is the width. BTW in the formulation above it should probably be
```
a, a_valid = util.unitize(triangles[:, 1] - triangles[:, 0], check_valid=True)
b, b_valid = util.unitize(triangles[:, 2] - triangles[:, 1], check_valid=True)
valid = np.logical_and(a_valid, b_valid)
```

Your point about preferring merged vertices over degenerate faces makes sense- so we should only be throwing away faces significantly smaller than tol.merge (tol_zero seems good).

I agree this is not ideal to have unavailability errors go straight up as unhandled exceptions.

`r.status_code == 503` already raises HTTPError with response attribute on which you can check the status code
```python
try:
    r.raise_for_status()
except Exception as e:
    # e.reponse.status_code == 503
```
`TooManyRequests` is quite provider specific, I'm not sure this is relevant to put this in a global function.
Sometimes `raise_for_status` isn't used because one expects a redirect or something like that.

I suggest a less invasive approach: catch HTTPError with 503 status code pretty much as ServiceUnavailable and leave the TooManyRequests handling on a provider basis.

FWIW, the sub expressions are left associative so you just need to grab the left most leaf node in the AST which will be the first field name.  Something like this (probably want some error checking to make sure that node is a `field` type):

```
>>> result = jmespath.compile('foo[].bar[].baz.*.qux')

> Just to clarify, you mean just for the method right? The comment above also mentioned about the headers, and while I agree with you that the ANY checks everywhere is weird, I'd also like it to be consistent.

Yes, the comment you replied to was just about the `e_method` check.

Okay, on to your other points...

First, I'm going to assume when you say "parameters" in this comment that you're talking about the items given in the tuples passed to `SequenceStringStubs`, not the parameters to `get_response_for`. 

> It seems necessary for params, because there needs to be some kind of way to distinguish between no params and any params

I read "It" as "explicitly checking `ANY`" here, but now I'm confused because we're _not_ explicitly checking `ANY` against `e_params`. If you meant headers instead of params (just a shot in the dark), then I still don't understand because the current code already doesn't give you a way to check "no headers" vs "any headers" -- passing `{}` or `ANY` both mean the same thing, which is "any headers", since we hardcode the use of `HasHeaders`.

> And its' easier to document that ANY can be used for all these values, instead of "the first parameter is required, the second and third parameters can be ANY, but for the fourth parameter {} represents ANY, and the fifth parameter can be ANY.

To pick on the point about parameter #4: it's not that `{}` "represents" `ANY` -- `ANY` is a just an unnecessary concept here since we've already got a perfectly good value that will always be a subset of the actual headers, and that is `{}`.

If we use `ANY` as the de facto sentinel, then okay, it makes sense to allow it for any element. But I'm trying to figure out a way to eradicate the explicit checks against `ANY` entirely.

>  (speaking of which do you think I should also accept None?)

I think this would be good, but I'd like to see if we can have `None` replace `ANY` entirely to mean "don't perform this check". That seems like it would be more idiomatic/unsurprising than giving `ANY` some special meaning. And I don't _think_ there's any overloading problems there, assuming that all the things we're checking have a reasonable "empty" value, which I'm pretty sure is true: params is `{}`, headers is `{}`, data is `""`. Or is there a meaningful distinction between a `data` of `""` and `data` of `None`?


## The important part of this comment:

(I considered just deleting everything I'd written before this part because I think this part pretty succinctly covers my position, but I'll leave that stuff there for posterity, and in case my proposal doesn't work out ;-)

I think it would be even nicer if we could avoid having a sentinel at all and just use ANY "naturally" for equality checks. I'm defining "naturally" as "only using it for its equality semantics". Lemme think about each field:

1. method - no problem, `ANY` can be used naturally
2. URL - URLPath has no equality method. wtf. and even if it did it may be annoying to require users to construct URLPath objects instead of just passing strings. Oh! But wait! Why can't we just make the check `e_url == str(URLPath.fromString(url))`? That would allow users to use `ANY` naturally. Why were we round-tripping the expected URL through URLPath?
3. params - no problem, users can use ANY naturally.
4. headers - since we're not using equality here, ANY is inappropriate, but `{}` has the desired meaning so users can just pass that.
5. data - no problem, `ANY` can be used naturally.


Okay, so unless there's something wrong with my proposal to not round-trip through URLPath, I think we can get rid of *any* use of an explicit "don't check this field" sentinel. And I don't think there's a documentation problem here -- we must just document that each field must be given. A friendly hint that `ANY` is a good way to not care about particular fields at the end of the docstring should be good enough.

But I'm sure I'm missing something :)

               foyer_fiscal('f6hk', period) +  # report année 2010
               foyer_fiscal('f6hl', period) +  # report année 2011
               foyer_fiscal('f6hm', period) +  # report année 2012
               foyer_fiscal('f6hn', period) +  # report année 2013

        )
   
        plafond_grosses_reparations = parameters(period).impot_revenu.charges_deductibles.grosses_reparations.plafond

                        foyer_fiscal('f6cb', period) +
                        np.where(period > '2009', foyer_fiscal('f6hj', period)) +  # report année 2009
                        np.where(period > '2010', foyer_fiscal('f6hk', period))  # report année 2010
                        np.where(period > '2011', foyer_fiscal('f6hl', period)) # report année 2011
                        np.where(period > '2012', foyer_fiscal('f6hm', period)) # report année 2012
                        np.where(period > '2013', foyer_fiscal('f6hn', period)) # report année 2013
                        np.where(period > '2014', foyer_fiscal('f6ho', period)) # report année 2014
                        np.where(period > '2015', foyer_fiscal('f6hp', period)) # report année 2015
        )

        plafond_grosses_reparations = parameters(period).impot_revenu.charges_deductibles.grosses_reparations.plafond

        return min_(somme_reparations, plafond_grosses_reparations)
```

@lukesneeringer I think my approach also preserves backwards compat, in that the same client library call will result in the same *permissions* but not the same *roles*. And nothing will explode with an error. 

The advantage of @tseaver 's approach is that it automatically upgrades users to the recommended behavior, the disadvantage is it prevents manipulating legacy roles (including removing) legacy roles. I think this is actually a big problem now that I think about it. Suppose you wanted to lock someone out of a pubsub resource, but they had a `roles/owners` membership.

```
policy = topic.get_iam_policy()
policy.owners.remove(member)
topic.set_iam_policy()
```

Doesn't actually do it! That said, neither does my approach, because it has the same problem with `roles/pubsub.admin`. However, my approach does allow the possibility of correcting this problem, via:

```
policy.owners.remove(member)
policy.admins.remove(member)
```

Again, we probably won't need to hardcode yet another version number here.

On a side note, the idea of "minimum recommended version" is intriguing. What do we want to convey here?

* If the intention is simply want to recommend customers to "always use whatever latest version is available" (which makes perfect sense), we can just write something like that, without hardcoding a specific version number.
* By definition of [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/), customers who using an old version X.Y.Z can always safely upgrade to newer version "X.Y-latest.Z-latest", without worrying about breaking change.
* Or customers can technically stay with a version "X.Y.Z", [as long as it still works](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/if_it_ain%27t_broke,_don%27t_fix_it).

I found this: https://cmake.org/Bug/view.php?id=7870
which led me to this: https://cmake.org/Wiki/CMake_MinGW_Compiler_Issues
And here's Appveyor's take: http://help.appveyor.com/discussions/problems/3193-cmake-building-for-mingw-issue-with-git-shexe

### Root Cause

Appveyor starts tests with an environment that includes GIT and everything it needs on your `PATH`.  This includes a copy of `sh.exe`.  Presumably, appveyor needs git to perform the project checkout, and they probably want to keep GIT on the `PATH` in case your project needs to run some other git commands.

The true root cause of the issue is MinGW Make insisting on running commands with `sh` if it's in your `PATH`, which will not work with Makefiles generated using the MinGW Makefile generator. The only way to force MinGW Make to run using `cmd.exe` is to explicitly remove any directory with an executable `sh` from the `PATH`.

### Fix in CMake?

Not wanting to meddle with a user's environment (let alone their `PATH`) seems like a good reason for a CMake maintainer to leave the issue as-is, especially considering that this is only a problem for Windows users who've deliberately added coreutils to their `PATH` (like in appveyor) and the simplest solution in this case is to just use a different generator.

### Fix in Appveyor?

On the other hand, keeping a functional git environment in a user's `PATH` from the outset seems like a more important feature to a member of the Appveyor support staff than catering to the specific needs of CMake users, or anyone who needs to use MinGW over Visual Studio, for that matter.

### What Do?

So, it's not obvious to me who we should be asking to free us from having to work around the issue, ourselves.

This won't work unfortunately. The change to /etc/environment will be persisted to the image (if one is made of the box). The creation of /mnt/ephemeral/temp will not. When a box is recreated from an image, it will likely fail because TMPDIR refers to an empty directory.

We get a similar effect by doing 

```
mkdir -p /mnt/ephemeral/tmp && export TMPDIR=/mnt/ephemeral/tmp
```

before every build in Jenkins. That's easy to do. In fact, I will do that right now for the Toil builds.

In the long run, having /tmp on ephemeral is generally beneficial, for all cgcloud boxes. It would have to be done in the same place where /mnt/ephemeral is prepared, CloudInitBox I think. Also, a bind mount would be better than overriding TMPDIR. That way, the logical path would remain /tmp while physically, things would end up in /mnt/ephemeral/tmp. We'd have to think about files already present in /tmp.

I don't think functions get dispatched here.  The dispatch entries for `save_global` are:

```python
     dispatch[type] = save_global
     dispatch[types.ClassType] = save_global
```

whereas function-likes are dispatched via:

```python
dispatch[types.FunctionType] = save_function
...
dispatch[types.MethodType] = save_instancemethod
...
dispatch[types.BuiltinFunctionType] = save_builtin_function
...
dispatch[classmethod] = save_classmethod
dispatch[staticmethod] = save_classmethod
```

I believe the name `save_global` is a holdover from the base `Pickler` class, which calls `self.save_global` in some base class methods that we call into via `super`, so renaming this isn't straightforward.

> Sticking a hash somewhere when we write and writing .new out immediately would have the same effect, I don't actually have a preference between them. I'm happy to write a hash out to some file somewhere.

If you write out the `.new` file at the same time as you write the live file it has the same effect. I have a preference for either including a special value/version in the file itself or creating an effectively hidden file containing the original contents/hash than to create the publicly visible `.new` file initially. If we did a hidden file, it could be an actually hidden file in `/etc/letsencrypt` or something placed somewhere like `/var/lib/letsencrypt` (although the latter opens us up to other problems I identified above).

 > Also, it occurs to me that we don't actually have to write out a .new -- we could just point them to constants.MOD_SSL_CONF_SRC.

Yeah that'd be fine too.

> I really don't think we should make lots of noise over and over until they tell us to shut up. Especially because they might update the contents of a field we changed, but have the file still be different from the updated version. Then, they'd have to tell us with a field or flag set somewhere, which I definitely don't want to be adding.

Very good point. Forcing the user to either take our file as is or turn off updates entirely is a bad idea. Basically we'd want some way to know the user is happy with the current state of the file, but I don't think there's really a clean way to do that. `--nginx-no-tls-updates-until-next-time` is pretty ridiculous.

> But, why is it enormous? 

The primary reasons it is enormous is because pulumi pulls in both grpc (56MB) and typescript (35mb).  Together, we're already close to 100MB on just these two packages.  These two packages are only needed by pulumi at deployment time to:

1. Use GRPC to communicate with teh pulumi engine (something not needed at cloud-runtime). 
2. Use TypeScript ot analyzer serialize a javascript function into the code needed by the AWS lambda (something not needed at cloud-runtime).

> And why is an enormous package specific in any way to @pulumi?

It's specific to pulumi because the @pulumi libraires are the ones that are so specifically intended primarily for deployment time. This is different form (literally) every other library out there, which very much *is* intended to be used at runtime by an application. 

It's also specific to @pulumi in that users have no control here about including those packages.  You have to include those packages, or else pulumi simply doesn't work.  However, any other packages you don't need, you can simply leave out of your package.json.  However, Pulumi Applications are themselves special in that they have two very distinct lifetimes.  The local deployment lifetime, and the remote cloud lifetime.  @pulumi is needed for the former, but we don't want the usage int he former to cost you for the latter. :)

> Yes, but the key word in here is much. There is some of it that could work.

Note: it's important to be very precise about what does or doesn't work here.  If you end up trying to use part of pulumi which *can* run in the cloud then it *will* work.  That's part of this work.  The poster-child for this is "pulumi.Config".  You can use Config inside the cloud because it is specifically accessing the part of the pulumi that will work, while side stepping the parts that will not work. 

Similarly, you can obviously capture and use any sort of pulumi resource (even though those came from an @pulumi module).  What does *not* work is trying to write code that effectively would become a `require("@pulumi")` at runtime.  So far, there are no scenarios we can envision where could would want to actually do that.

So what happens now is that parameters of the form

`parameter Real a = 1.0;`

get their numerical value exposed.

OTOH parameters like

`parameter Real b = a;`

do not. The original intent was to produce the value `1.0` when accessing `b` as well.

So this is an API change which will have ramifications for RTC-Tools as well as some applications. For instance, now, any calculations in `pre()` using parameter values may fail if there is an in-between assignment anywhere.
